Axitinib Versus Sorafenib in First-Line Metastatic Renal Cell Carcinoma: Overall Survival From a Randomized Phase III Trial
Thomas E. Hutson,¹ Salman Al-Shukri,² Viktor P. Stus,³ Oleg N. Lipatov,⁴ Yaroslav Shparyk,⁵ Angel H. Bair,⁶ Brad Rosbrook,⁶ Glen I. Andrews,⁶ Nicholas J. Vogelzang⁷

# Abstract In a randomized phase III trial, axitinib did not significantly improve progression-free survival over sorafenib in treatment-naive patients with metastatic renal cell carcinoma. This updated analysis confirmed clinical activity of axitinib with an acceptable safety profile after long-term treatment. Although axitinib did not prolong survival over sorafenib, axitinib might provide an acceptable first-line treatment option in patients with good performance status.

**Background:** In a randomized phase III trial in treatment-naive patients with metastatic renal cell carcinoma (RCC), axitinib versus sorafenib yielded numerically longer progression-free survival (median, 10.1 vs. 6.5 months; hazard ratio [HR], 0.77; 1-sided P = .038) and significantly higher objective response rate (32% vs. 15%; 1-sided P = .0006). In this article, we report overall survival (OS) and updated safety results. **Patients and Methods:** Previously untreated patients with metastatic RCC (n = 288), stratified according to Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status (ECOG PS; 0 vs. 1), were randomized 2:1 to receive axitinib 5 mg twice per day (b.i.d.; n = 192) or sorafenib 400 mg b.i.d. (n = 96). **Results:** Median OS (95% confidence interval [CI]) was 21.7 months (18.0-31.7) with axitinib versus 23.3 months (18.1-33.2) with sorafenib (stratified HR, 0.995; 95% CI, 0.731-1.356; 1-sided P = .4883). Among patients with ECOG PS of 0, median OS was numerically longer with axitinib than with sorafenib (41.2 vs. 31.9 months; HR, 0.811, 1-sided P = .1748), whereas among patients with ECOG PS 1, median OS was shorter with axitinib than with sorafenib (14.2 vs. 19.8 months; HR, 1.203; 1-sided; P = .7973). Incidence and severity of common adverse events were consistent with previous reports. **Conclusion:** OS was similar between axitinib and sorafenib in treatment-naive patients with metastatic RCC, and no new safety signals emerged.

*Clinical Genitourinary Cancer*, Vol. 15, No. 1, 72-6  2016 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved. Keywords: Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status, First-line treatment, Randomized phase III trial, Tyrosine kinase inhibitor, VEGF receptor inhibitor

objective response rate was, however, significantly higher with axitinib versus sorafenib (32% vs. 15%; 1-sided P = .0006). Furthermore, a predefined subgroup analysis showed that PFS was longer with axitinib than with sorafenib in patients with an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status (ECOG PS) of 0 but not in those with an ECOG PS of 1. Common ( 30% of patients) all-causality, all-grade adverse events (AEs) included diarrhea, hypertension, weight decrease, and fatigue with axitinib treatment, and diarrhea and palmar-plantar eryth- rodysesthesia with sorafenib treatment. In this article, we report overall survival (OS) and updated safety results from this phase III study.

# Patients and Methods The details of this randomized, open-label phase III study (ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT00920816), conducted in compliance with the Declaration of Helsinki, the International Conference on Harmonisation guidelines on Good Clinical Practice, and other requirements, have been reported previously.² Briefly, treatment-naive patients with confirmed clear-cell mRCC, measurable disease per Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors version 1.0, ECOG PS 0 or 1, and blood pressure ≤ 140/90 mm Hg were eligible for the study. Patients were stratified according to ECOG PS and randomly assigned (2:1), using a centralized registration system, to receive twice-daily axitinib 5 mg or sorafenib 400 mg orally in 4-week cycles. The axitinib dose could be up- or down-titrated under predefined clinical conditions, whereas the sorafenib dose could only be reduced. The primary end point of this study, PFS assessed by a blinded independent review committee, has been reported²; OS was a secondary end point.

Radiologic tumor assessments were performed at screening, weeks 6 and 12, and every 8 weeks thereafter. Safety was monitored throughout the study, and AEs were graded per Common Termi- nology Criteria for Adverse Events version 3.0. Patients were fol- lowed for survival every 3 months after discontinuing study treatment until at least 3 years after randomization of the last patient.

The estimated sample size of approximately 247 patients, which was required for this study to have 90% power to detect a difference in PFS (overall 1-sided α = 0.025), was also sufficient to have 80% power to detect a difference in OS after 178 deaths, assuming an approximately 59% improvement in OS with axiti- nib versus sorafenib (median OS, 27 vs. 17 months), a follow-up period of approximately 37 months, and no dropout. OS in each treatment arm was analyzed with the Kaplan—Meier method and compared with a 1-sided log-rank test, stratified according to ECOG PS.

## Results A total of 288 treatment-naive patients with mRCC were randomly assigned to the axitinib (n = 192) or sorafenib (n = 96) arm.² Three patients in the axitinib arm did not receive treatment and were excluded from the safety analysis. At the time of the data cutoff date for OS analysis (December 18, 2014), 161 (84%) patients in the axitinib arm versus 83 (86%) in the sor-afenib arm discontinued study treatment, mostly because of

objective progression or disease relapse (50% vs. 63%), followed by deaths (11% vs. 8%), refusal of treatment for reason other than AE (8% vs. 3%), and AEs (6% vs. 4%; Figure 1). After discontinuation of study treatment, comparable proportions of patients in each treatment arm received follow-up systemic treatments (Table 1).

In all, 115 (60%) and 62 (65%) patients in the axitinib and sorafenib arm, respectively, died mainly from disease progression, of whom 38 (20%) and 10 (10%) patients, respectively, died while receiving treatment. Median OS (95% CI) was 21.7 months (18.0-31.7) with axitinib compared with 23.3 months (18.1- 33.2) with sorafenib (stratified HR, 0.995; 95% CI, 0.731-1.356; 1-sided P .4883; Figure 2A). In the ECOG PS 0 subgroup, axitinib showed improved survival compared with sorafenib, but this was not observed in the ECOG PS 1 subgroup (Figure 2B and C).

Common (≥ 30% of patients) treatment-emergent, all-causality AEs were diarrhea, hypertension, weight decrease, and fatigue with axitinib, and diarrhea, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, and hypertension with sorafenib (Table 2). Among the common all-grade AEs, AEs reported more frequently (≥ 10%) with axitinib than with sorafenib were diarrhea, hypertension, weight decrease, decreased appetite, dysphonia, and hypothyroidism; those more frequently reported with sorafenib than with axitinib were palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia and rash. Grade ≥ 3 hypertension was more common with axitinib than with sorafenib, as was weight decrease and fatigue, whereas Grade ≥ 3 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia was reported more frequently with sorafenib. Diarrhea was reported as the most common all-causality serious AE in 7 (4%) axitinib-treated patients and in 2 (2%) sorafenib-treated patients.

# Discussion This randomized phase III trial showed that OS was similar between axitinib and sorafenib in the first-line treatment of mRCC. In the predefined subgroup analysis, a 9.3-month difference in median OS with axitinib over sorafenib was observed in patients with ECOG PS 0, but the difference was in favor of sorafenib in patients with ECOG PS 1. A similar trend favoring axitinib over sorafenib in patients with ECOG PS 0, but no difference in patients with ECOG PS 1, was reported for PFS, the primary end point of the study, in the primary analysis. Within each treatment arm, median OS was longer in patients with ECOG PS 0 versus ECOG PS 1 (axitinib, 41.2 vs. 14.2 months; sorafenib, 31.9 vs. 19.8 months). Although ECOG PS is one of the prognostic factors identified for PFS and OS in patients with RCC treated with VEGF pathway-targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs)³, it is somewhat subjective in nature. It is unclear if the results of the ECOG PS subgroup analyses in our study indicate a preferential effect of axitinib over sorafenib in patients with more favorable prognosis, and further investigation is warranted.

The actual median OS for axitinib (21.7 months) in this study was shorter than predicted (27 months), whereas actual median OS for sorafenib (23.3 months) was longer than predicted (17 months). Although cross-study comparison should be interpreted with caution, the observed median OS values were at the shorter

tivozanib versus sorafenib, in which only a trend toward longer survival with sorafenib was seen over tivozanib (29.3 vs. 28.8 months). OS might also be affected by factors such as crossover between treatment arms and/or use of follow-up therapies. In the current study, however, crossover was not permitted and a similar percentage of patients (axitinib, 18%; sorafenib, 23%) received follow-up therapies. It is unclear why median OS for axitinib and sorafenib was shorter in this study than in other studies, but reasons would likely be multifactorial. It is noteworthy that the use of second- and subsequent-line therapies in this study was considerably lower than in other studies, in which > 50% of patients received post-study follow-up therapies, 4,8,11 perhaps reflecting regional differences in standard clinical practice. In this study, 51% of patients were enrolled from Eastern European countries, where targeted therapies might not be reimbursed or covered by insurance, which could partially account for less frequent use of subsequent therapies, including targeted therapies.

As discussed in the primary report,² some limitations of the study included a relatively small sample size, sorafenib as a comparator instead of sunitinib or pazopanib, which are standard therapies for first-line treatment of mRCC, and a relatively large proportion of patients recruited from countries with limited access to subsequent targeted therapies. In addition, patients and investigators were not blinded to treatment, which could have biased safety assessments.
The safety profile of axitinib or sorafenib in this updated analyses was generally similar to those reported in the primary analysis² and

tolerated. Therefore, further clinical evaluation of axitinib as first-line therapy for mRCC is warranted.

### Clinical Practice Points * Axitinib, a potent and selective inhibitor of VEGF receptors 1-3, is approved by the FDA for treatment of patients with previously treated mRCC. * In a randomized phase III trial, which evaluated the efficacy and safety of axitinib compared with sorafenib in treatment-naive patients with mRCC, clinical activity of axitinib was shown, although the difference in the primary end point of PFS did not reach the predefined significance. * In the current updated analysis, axitinib did not prolong survival compared with sorafenib, but clinical activity of axitinib in pa- tients with previously untreated mRCC was confirmed and its safety after long-term treatment was shown. * The overall results of this randomized phase III trial provides supporting evidence for further clinical development of axitinib in first-line treatment of mRCC. * The landscape for treatment of patients with mRCC is changing rapidly with several available targeted therapies, including axiti- nib. Long-term safety and efficacy data might be useful in guiding treatment decisions for patients with advanced RCC.

At the time of the data cutoff date for OS analysis (December 18, 2014), 161 (84%) patients in the axitinib arm versus 83 (86%) in the sor-afenib arm discontinued study treatment, mostly because of objective progression or disease relapse (50% vs. 63%), followed by deaths (11% vs. 8%), refusal of treatment for reason other than AE (8% vs. 3%), and AEs (6% vs. 4%; Figure 1). After discontinuation of study treatment, comparable proportions of patients in each treatment arm received follow-up systemic treatments (Table 1). In all, 115 (60%) and 62 (65%) patients in the axitinib and sorafenib arm, respectively, died mainly from disease progression, of whom 38 (20%) and 10 (10%) patients, respectively, died while receiving treatment. In the ECOG PS 0 subgroup, axitinib showed improved survival compared with sorafenib, but this was not observed in the ECOG PS 1 subgroup (Figure 2B and C). Common (≥ 30% of patients) treatment-emergent, all-causality AEs were diarrhea, hypertension, weight decrease, and fatigue with axitinib, and diarrhea, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, and hypertension with sorafenib (Table 2). Among the common all-grade AEs, AEs reported more frequently (≥ 10%) with axitinib than with sorafenib were diarrhea, hypertension, weight decrease, decreased appetite, dysphonia, and hypothyroidism; those more frequently reported with sorafenib than with axitinib were palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia and rash. Grade ≥ 3 hypertension was more common with axitinib than with sorafenib, as was weight decrease and fatigue, whereas Grade ≥ 3 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia was reported more frequently with sorafenib. A similar trend favoring axitinib over sorafenib in patients with ECOG PS 0, but no difference in patients with ECOG PS 1, was reported for PFS, the primary end point of the study, in the primary analysis. Within each treatment arm, median OS was longer in patients with ECOG PS 0 versus ECOG PS 1 (axitinib, 41.2 vs. 14.2 months; sorafenib, 31.9 vs. 19.8 months). Although ECOG PS is one of the prognostic factors identified for PFS and OS in patients with RCC treated with VEGF pathway-targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs)³, it is somewhat subjective in nature. It is unclear if the results of the ECOG PS subgroup analyses in our study indicate a preferential effect of axitinib over sorafenib in patients with more favorable prognosis, and further investigation is warranted. The actual median OS for axitinib (21.7 months) in this study was shorter than predicted (27 months), whereas actual median OS for sorafenib (23.3 months) was longer than predicted (17 months). It is noteworthy that the use of second- and subsequent-line therapies in this study was considerably lower than in other studies, in which > 50% of patients received post-study follow-up therapies, 4,8,11 perhaps reflecting regional differences in standard clinical practice. In this study, 51% of patients were enrolled from Eastern European countries, where targeted therapies might not be reimbursed or covered by insurance, which could partially account for less frequent use of subsequent therapies, including targeted therapies. As discussed in the primary report,² some limitations of the study included a relatively small sample size, sorafenib as a comparator instead of sunitinib or pazopanib, which are standard therapies for first-line treatment of mRCC, and a relatively large proportion of patients recruited from countries with limited access to subsequent targeted therapies. In addition, patients and investigators were not blinded to treatment, which could have biased safety assessments. Therefore, further clinical evaluation of axitinib as first-line therapy for mRCC is warranted. ### Clinical Practice Points * Axitinib, a potent and selective inhibitor of VEGF receptors 1-3, is approved by the FDA for treatment of patients with previously treated mRCC. * In a randomized phase III trial, which evaluated the efficacy and safety of axitinib compared with sorafenib in treatment-naive patients with mRCC, clinical activity of axitinib was shown, although the difference in the primary end point of PFS did not reach the predefined significance. * In the current updated analysis, axitinib did not prolong survival compared with sorafenib, but clinical activity of axitinib in pa- tients with previously untreated mRCC was confirmed and its safety after long-term treatment was shown. * The overall results of this randomized phase III trial provides supporting evidence for further clinical development of axitinib in first-line treatment of mRCC. * The landscape for treatment of patients with mRCC is changing rapidly with several available targeted therapies, including axiti- nib. Long-term safety and efficacy data might be useful in guiding treatment decisions for patients with advanced RCC. ## Acknowledgments This study was sponsored by Pfizer.
